meta.title: Mercedes-Benz M275/M279 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M275/M279 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M275 and M279 are the ultimate modern V12 biturbos — immense power and refinement, but complex and ruinously expensive to fix without specialist care. Backed by real UK enquiries [ESTIMATED].

verdictRating.confidence: 78% confidence — based on owner reports and specialist workshop feedback across the modern V12 range.

rows.score: 8 (Complex V12 biturbo, specialist-only; many jobs require engine-out access)
12 (Parts available but dealer-only for many components; expensive)
4 (Recon cost ~£8,000 against lowest host value ~£8,000 = 100% (75%+ band → 4))

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: "The M275/279 is a magnificent flagship V12 — but repair costs can exceed the car's value on older examples — buy only with full history and a specialist relationship."
verdictRating.bestFor: Connoisseurs of ultimate Mercedes V12 luxury who can afford specialist maintenance and preventative care.
verdictRating.avoidIf: You're on a tight budget, or looking at a high-mileage example without documented coil pack and ABC system history.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M275/M279 typically takes 16–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [ESTIMATED].

cta.label: → Compare M275/M279 engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: Cost — "How much does an M275/M279 engine replacement cost?"
items.answer: A reconditioned M275/M279 typically costs £6,000–£10,000 supply only [ESTIMATED], varying by displacement (5.5L vs 6.0L) and AMG specification. Fitting adds £1,400–£2,800 depending on labour rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

items.question: Reliability — "Is the M275/M279 a reliable engine?"
items.answer: The M275 and M279 are robust for V12 biturbos when properly maintained — many examples exceed 150,000 miles. However, coil pack failure, ignition module issues, and ABC suspension-linked components are common and expensive. Preventative maintenance is essential.

items.question: Common failure — "What is the most common M275/M279 failure?"
items.answer: Coil pack and ignition module failure is the most frequent issue — each cylinder has its own coil, and failure typically presents as misfires, rough running, or limp mode. Turbocharger oil seals and cooling system components (water pump, radiator) are also common failure points on higher-mileage examples.

items.question: Fitment — "Which Mercedes-Benz models use the M275/M279?"
items.answer: The M275 powered the S600, CL600, SL600, and G65 AMG from the mid-2000s onward. The M279 is the updated 6.0L version, fitted to later S65, SL65, and G65 models from approximately 2012.

items.question: Longevity — "How long does the M275/M279 last?"
items.answer: With meticulous maintenance — especially regular ignition system checks and cooling system servicing — M275/M279 engines can exceed 150,000–200,000 miles. However, neglect leads to very expensive repairs, so many examples are scrapped early due to repair costs exceeding vehicle value.
